title: Statistics & Data
blurb: Master mean, median, mode, range, weighted averages, spread, percentiles, the normal curve, and data reading.
theory:
The Big Idea

Statistics on the GRE is about summarizing a list of numbers with a few key measures: where the data centers (mean, median, mode), how spread out it is (range, standard deviation, IQR), and where individual values rank (percentiles, quartiles). Most questions reward solid arithmetic and a clear understanding of definitions far more than heavy calculation.

Mean (Average)

The mean is the sum of all values divided by how many there are.

mean = (sum of values) ÷ (number of values)

The single most useful rearrangement on the GRE is:

total = average × count

Whenever a problem gives you an average, immediately convert it to a total. Averages do not add, but totals do. If the average of 5 numbers is 30, their total is 30 × 5 = 150.

Median

The median is the middle value once the data is sorted in order. You MUST sort first.

  • If the count is odd, the median is the single middle value. For n values, it is at position (n + 1) ÷ 2.
  • If the count is even, the median is the average of the two middle values.

For the sorted set 3, 8, 8, 12, 15, 20 (six values), the two middle values are 8 and 12, so the median is (8 + 12) ÷ 2 = 10.

The median is resistant to extreme values (outliers); the mean is not. A single huge value can pull the mean way up while barely moving the median.

Mode

The mode is the value that appears most often. A set can have no mode, one mode, or several. In 5, 7, 7, 9, 9, 9, 12 the mode is 9 (it appears three times).

Range

The range = largest value − smallest value. It is the crudest measure of spread and depends entirely on the two extremes.

Weighted Average

When groups have different sizes (or weights), you cannot simply average the averages. Multiply each value by its weight, add those products, then divide by the total weight.

weighted average = (sum of value × weight) ÷ (sum of weights)

GroupAverageCount (weight)Total
Class A78251950
Class B90151350

Combined average = (1950 + 1350) ÷ (25 + 15) = 3300 ÷ 40 = 82.5. Notice it is closer to 78 than to 90 because Class A has more students. A weighted average always lands closer to the value with the larger weight.

Effect of Adding or Removing a Value

Use totals. If a new value is greater than the current mean, the mean rises; if less, it falls; if equal, it stays the same. The median may shift in less obvious ways because it depends on position, not size.

Standard Deviation (Spread)

The standard deviation (SD) measures how far, on average, the values sit from the mean. A small SD means values cluster tightly near the mean; a large SD means they are widely scattered. You will almost never compute SD by hand on the GRE. Instead, you compare spreads conceptually.

  • Sets {75, 76, 77, 78, 79} and {60, 70, 80, 90, 100} have very different spreads — the second has a much larger SD.
  • Adding the same constant to every value shifts the mean but leaves the SD unchanged.
  • Multiplying every value by a positive constant k multiplies the SD by k.
  • Adding a value equal to the mean reduces the SD slightly; adding an extreme value increases it.

Quartiles & Interquartile Range

Sorting the data and splitting it into four equal parts gives the quartiles. Q1 (first quartile) is the median of the lower half, Q2 is the overall median, and Q3 (third quartile) is the median of the upper half. The interquartile range (IQR) = Q3 − Q1 captures the spread of the middle 50% of the data and ignores extreme values.

Percentiles

A value at the p-th percentile is greater than or equal to p% of the data. Scoring in the 80th percentile means you scored at least as high as 80% of test-takers (20% scored higher). Percentiles describe rank, not a raw score.

The Normal Distribution & the 68-95-99.7 Rule

Many data sets follow a bell-shaped normal distribution, symmetric about the mean. For such distributions:

  • About 68% of values fall within 1 SD of the mean.
  • About 95% fall within 2 SD of the mean.
  • About 99.7% fall within 3 SD of the mean.

Because the curve is symmetric, half of each band lies on each side. So about 34% lie between the mean and +1 SD. The percentage above the mean + 1 SD is (100 − 68) ÷ 2 = 16%.

Reading Tables, Bar, and Line Graphs

Data Interpretation sets present a chart or table followed by several questions. Read the title, axis labels, and units first. Common pitfalls: confusing percent change with absolute change, misreading the scale, and overlooking units (thousands vs millions). Always ground each answer in the exact numbers shown.
whatEtsTests:
• total = average × count as the workhorse for nearly every average problem, especially adding, removing, or replacing values.
  • Median vs. mean, and recognizing that the median resists outliers while the mean does not.
  • Weighted averages disguised as ordinary averages (different group sizes).
  • Standard deviation tested conceptually as spread in Quantitative Comparison — comparing two sets without computing.
  • The 68-95-99.7 rule for the normal distribution, often asking for the percent above or below a given number of SDs.
  • Percentiles and quartiles/IQR definitions, plus careful reading of tables, bar graphs, and line graphs in multi-question Data Interpretation sets.
tips: Convert every average into a total immediately (total = average × count). Totals add and subtract cleanly; averages do not.
Always sort the list before finding the median, and count the values to decide odd (single middle) vs even (average of two middles).
For a weighted average, the answer is always pulled toward the value with the larger weight — use this to eliminate impossible choices.
To compare standard deviations by eye, look at how tightly the values cluster around the mean; ignore the actual mean values.
Adding the same number to every value leaves the spread/SD unchanged; adding a value equal to the mean shrinks it.
For normal-distribution questions, sketch the bell curve and split 68/95/99.7 in half on each side to read off percentages fast.
traps: Forgetting to sort before taking the median, or miscounting odd vs even and grabbing the wrong middle value.
Averaging the averages when group sizes differ — this ignores the weights and gives a wrong simple average.
Assuming a larger range means a larger standard deviation; SD depends on all values, not just the two extremes.
Confusing percentile (rank) with percentage score, e.g. treating an 80th-percentile finish as an 80% score.
On the normal curve, forgetting the distribution is symmetric and using a full band (e.g. 68%) when only one tail or half is asked.

examples.q: The average (arithmetic mean) of 6 numbers is 82. A 7th number is added so that the average of all 7 numbers becomes 84. What is the value of the 7th number?
examples.steps:
1. Convert each average to a total. Original total = 82 × 6 = 492.
  2. New total = 84 × 7 = 588.
  3. The 7th number is the difference: 588 − 492 = 96.
examples.shortcut: Adding a value that raises the average of 7 numbers by 2 means the new value carries its own 84 plus 2 extra for each of the other 6: 84 + 6 × 2 = 96.

examples.q: A student scored 80 on each of 3 tests and 92 on each of 2 tests. What is the student's average score across all 5 tests?
examples.steps:
1. This is a weighted average. Find the total points: (80 × 3) + (92 × 2) = 240 + 184 = 424.
  2. Divide by the total number of tests: 424 ÷ 5 = 84.8.
examples.shortcut: The two values are 12 apart. The mean sits 2/5 of the way from 80 toward 92: 80 + (2/5)(12) = 84.8.

examples.q: Set X = {60, 70, 80, 90, 100} and Set Y = {75, 76, 77, 78, 79}. Without computing, which set has the greater standard deviation, and why?
examples.steps:
1. Standard deviation measures how far values lie from their mean.
  2. Set X stretches from 60 to 100 — values sit far from the center.
  3. Set Y is packed within 4 units — values sit very close to the center.
  4. Therefore Set X has the greater standard deviation because its values are much more spread out.
examples.shortcut: Compare the clustering visually: Y is a tight cluster, X is widely scattered. Tighter cluster = smaller SD.

examples.q: Scores on a test are normally distributed with mean 500 and standard deviation 100. Approximately what percent of scores are above 600?
examples.steps:
1. 600 is exactly 1 standard deviation above the mean (500 + 100).
  2. By the 68-95-99.7 rule, about 68% of scores fall within 1 SD of the mean (between 400 and 600).
  3. The remaining 100 − 68 = 32% lies in the two tails, split equally by symmetry.
  4. So the percent above 600 is 32 ÷ 2 = 16%.
examples.shortcut: Above mean + 1 SD is always about 16% for a normal distribution; memorize this single number.

questions.q: What is the average (arithmetic mean) of 4, 7, 11, 18, and 20?
What is the median of the data set 3, 8, 8, 12, 15, 20?
questions.explain: The set is already sorted and has 6 (even) values, so the median is the average of the two middle values, 8 and 12: (8 + 12) ÷ 2 = 10.

questions.q: What is the range of the data set 14, 9, 22, 7, 30?
The average (arithmetic mean) of 5 numbers is 30. Four of the numbers are 25, 28, 32, and 35. What is the value of the fifth number?
questions.explain: Total of all five = 30 × 5 = 150. The four known numbers sum to 25 + 28 + 32 + 35 = 120. The fifth number = 150 − 120 = 30.

questions.q: A class of 25 students has an average score of 78, and a class of 15 students has an average score of 90. What is the average score of all 40 students combined?
questions.explain: Use totals: (25 × 78) + (15 × 90) = 1950 + 1350 = 3300. Combined average = 3300 ÷ 40 = 82.5. It is closer to 78 because the 25-student class carries more weight.

questions.q: Scores on an exam are normally distributed with mean 70 and standard deviation 5. Approximately what percent of scores fall between 60 and 80?
questions.explain: 60 is 2 SD below the mean (70 − 2 × 5) and 80 is 2 SD above (70 + 2 × 5). By the 68-95-99.7 rule, about 95% of values fall within 2 standard deviations of the mean.

questions.q: A set of 6 numbers has an average (arithmetic mean) of 15. When one number is removed, the average of the remaining 5 numbers is 16. What is the value of the number that was removed?
questions.explain: Original total = 15 × 6 = 90. Remaining total = 16 × 5 = 80. Removed value = 90 − 80 = 10. (Removing a below-average value raises the mean, which is consistent.)

questions.q: Set P = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50} and Set Q = {28, 29, 30, 31, 32}. Both sets have a mean of 30.
questions.info: Set P = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50} and Set Q = {28, 29, 30, 31, 32}. Both sets have a mean of 30.
questions.qa: The standard deviation of Set P
questions.qb: The standard deviation of Set Q
questions.explain: Both sets share the same mean of 30, so compare spread. Set P's values stretch from 10 to 50 (far from the mean), while Set Q's values cluster within 2 units of the mean. The more widely spread set, P, has the greater standard deviation. Quantity A is greater.

questions.q: The data set 1, 3, 5, 7, 9 has a mean of 5 and a median of 5. The value 100 is added to the set. Which of the following statements are true about the new 6-value set? Select all that apply.
choices: The mean is greater than the median
questions.explain: New set sorted: 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 100. New mean = 125 ÷ 6 ≈ 20.8, so the mean increases (true). New median = (5 + 7) ÷ 2 = 6, up from 5, so the median increases (true). Mean ≈ 20.8 is greater than median 6 (true). Range was 8 and is now 99, so it does not stay the same (false). All values appear once, so there is no mode of 100 (false).

questions.q: A list of 9 distinct numbers has a mean of 50. The largest number, 80, is removed from the list.
questions.info: A list of 9 distinct numbers has a mean of 50. The largest number, 80, is removed from the list.
questions.qa: The mean of the remaining 8 numbers
questions.explain: Original total = 50 × 9 = 450. After removing 80, remaining total = 450 − 80 = 370, and the new mean = 370 ÷ 8 = 46.25. Removing a value above the mean (80 > 50) always lowers the mean, so the remaining mean is less than 50. Quantity B is greater.

questions.q: The lengths (in cm) of 9 rods, in order, are 3, 5, 7, 8, 12, 13, 14, 18, 21. What is the interquartile range (IQR) of this data set?
questions.explain: The overall median is the 5th value, 12, which splits the data into a lower half (3, 5, 7, 8) and an upper half (13, 14, 18, 21). Q1 = median of the lower half = (5 + 7) ÷ 2 = 6. Q3 = median of the upper half = (14 + 18) ÷ 2 = 16. IQR = Q3 − Q1 = 16 − 6 = 10.

questions.q: The weights of newborns at a hospital are normally distributed with a mean of 3.4 kg and a standard deviation of 0.4 kg. A newborn is considered low birth weight if it weighs less than 2.6 kg. Approximately what percent of newborns are low birth weight?
questions.explain: 2.6 kg is 2 standard deviations below the mean (3.4 − 2 × 0.4 = 2.6). By the 68-95-99.7 rule, about 95% of values fall within 2 SD, leaving 5% in the two tails. By symmetry, half of that lies below mean − 2 SD: 5 ÷ 2 = 2.5%.
